paragraphs.text: TEK Norge, the Norwegian ICT and technology industry association, welcomes the opportunity to contribute to the European Commissions consultation on the forthcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA). We represent a broad range of technology companies that are directly affected by European digital regulation, and we are committed to a Europe that is competitive, innovative, and fair. General position While we support the Commissions ambition to ensure fairness in the digital economy and protect consumers, we strongly caution against introducing yet another horizontal regulation on top of the already complex digital regulatory landscape. The EU has in recent years adopted major frameworks such as the Digital Services Act (DSA), the Digital Markets Act (DMA), the Data Act, and the AI Act. Adding the DFA risks over-regulating the sector, creating overlaps, uncertainty, and increased compliance costs without delivering clear additional benefits. Our key concerns Risk of regulatory overload The accumulation of regulations risks making Europe a less attractive place for digital innovation and investment. European technology companies, including SMEs, are already devoting significant resources to compliance with the DSA, DMA and AI Act. Another horizontal law will increase costs, complexity, and legal uncertainty, weakening Europes global competitiveness. Duplications and overlaps Many of the issues the DFA seeks to address are already covered under existing frameworks. For example, consumer protection in digital markets is regulated through the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD), the Consumer Rights Directive (CRD), and recently strengthened via the Omnibus Directive. Further regulation may duplicate obligations rather than strengthen enforcement. Need for consistent enforcement rather than new rules The real challenge in Europe is not the lack of rules but the lack of effective enforcement. The Commission and Member States should focus on ensuring consistent, effective, and proportionate enforcement of the extensive body of digital legislation already in place. Introducing yet another law risks diverting attention and resources away from this core task. Impact on innovation and competitiveness Over-regulation disproportionately harms smaller companies, start-ups and scale-ups that lack large compliance departments. Europe is already lagging behind the US and Asia in digital innovation, venture capital, and scaling power. The DFA, as currently framed, risks widening this gap. Recommendations The Commission should carefully assess whether the DFA is necessary, or whether the objectives can be met through stronger enforcement of existing frameworks. Any new initiative must avoid duplication and clearly define its added value. The regulatory burden for SMEs must be minimized; proportionality should be a guiding principle. Europe must strike a balance between consumer protection and competitiveness. Excessive regulation undermines the ability of European companies to grow, compete and deliver innovative services. Conclusion TEK Norge urges the European Commission to reconsider the need for a new horizontal legislative act in the digital space. We support fairness and consumer protection, but believe the main priority should be consistent enforcement of existing rules, ensuring legal clarity, and fostering a competitive environment for European digital companies. Only through such an approach can Europe achieve both fairness and global competitiveness in the digital economy. yours sincerely, Pål Wien Espen, lawyer, TEK Norge
